evidence_quotes.quote: the company can alter their Terms at any time, for any reason, at its sole discretion, and doesn't consider it obligatory to notify the customer of the changes
This is an extremely customer-hostile practice

the customer is expected to agree to the current Terms and ANY FUTURE VERSION of them, which is essentially agreeing to something that cannot be specified, and that realistically cannot be the basis of a binding contract.
The binding version of the contract for each consumer should be the last version that the consumer agreed to.
Legal consequences for the companies who breach their own contract.

paragraphs.text: I think a major problem that we as consumers face is the "rules for thee, but not for me" approach from countless companies when it comes to their Terms of Service and similar legal contracts. It happens far too often that the company can alter their Terms at any time, for any reason, at its sole discretion, and doesn't consider it obligatory to notify the customer of the changes, making it instead the customer's impossible responsibility to play detective and regularly check for changes. This has been done even by major players like Mojang, who changed their legal contracts 47 times without ever making it known (source: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1-C5nEFaTJgB7juzf3BytBBoWGLFAM6WIUDSAvU2Vd3Y/edit?gid=2023714430#gid=2023714430), even going as far as removing the note showing the date of the latest update, which makes keeping up with the changes a practically impossible task. This is an extremely customer-hostile practice and it gets even more absurd when the customer is expected to agree to the current Terms and ANY FUTURE VERSION of them, which is essentially agreeing to something that cannot be specified, and that realistically cannot be the basis of a binding contract. When a company DOES inform the customer of the changes to their Terms, it often makes any further use of the service or product impossible unless the new Terms are accepted, which isn't much of a real choice. Again, Mojang/Microsoft are an example of such practice. Players who bought Minecraft and then were forced to migrate their Mojang account to a Microsoft account were faced with a "choice" where they would either agree to possibly unwanted changes under the threat of losing the product they bought, or they would disagree to the changes by terminating their account and consequently lose access to the game. What's more, many players lost access to their game simply because they were never notified by Mojang of the important changes to their account and didn't know that they had to migrate their account in order to keep the game, because that wasn't part of the agreement they had signed upon purchasing the game. Behaviors like those are very clearly against consumer protection laws and should have never become general business practice, yet they have. So, to ensure more transparency on the companies' side, I believe that the Digital Fairness Act should enforce the following: 1) The legal contract should be easily available in its entirety and in all its previous versions as well. 2) The company should always notify the consumer about the changes to the contract and give them the true choice of accepting the new Terms or sticking with the old ones. The consumer should not be forced to agree to changes under the threat of terminating the contract. 3) The binding version of the contract for each consumer should be the last version that the consumer agreed to. 4) Legal consequences for the companies who breach their own contract. If the consumer can be held accountable, so should the company. This should hopefully force the companies to formulate their Terms of Service more carefully and ensure that both sides are equally responsible for adhering to the contract. Companies have been abusing consumer rights without consequences for far too long.
